preamble.summary: Proverbs 12 advances the Solomonic collection with twenty-eight antithetic proverbs centered on the practical consequences of speech, work, and character. The chapter gives sustained attention to the tongue — honest versus deceitful speech, reckless versus healing words — and to the value of diligent labor over laziness. It also introduces the theme of how people handle animals, making the care of livestock a moral indicator.
preamble.remarkable: Verse 10 stands out as one of the earliest ethical statements about animal welfare in ancient literature: the righteous person knows the nefesh ('life, being') of his animal. This is not sentimentality but recognition that how a person treats creatures under his authority reveals his character. The chapter also contains the striking claim that truthful speech endures forever while lying lasts only a moment (verse 19) — a statement about the structural durability of truth versus the fragility of deception.
preamble.friction: The sharp binary between the diligent and the lazy (verses 11, 24, 27) reflects an agrarian economy where hard physical work was the primary path to survival. Modern economic realities are more complex — systemic factors can override individual effort. The proverbs describe tendencies, not universal laws. Likewise, the claim that no harm befalls the righteous (verse 21) states an ideal that the rest of Scripture — and human experience — frequently complicates.
preamble.connections: The 'tree of life' language from 11:30 continues implicitly through the agricultural imagery here. The concern with honest speech connects to the ninth commandment (Exodus 20:16). The righteous person's care for animals in verse 10 echoes Deuteronomy 25:4 ('you shall not muzzle an ox while it treads grain') and anticipates Jesus's argument from animal care in Luke 13:15.

verses.text_hebrew: אֹהֵ֣ב מ֭וּסָר אֹהֵ֣ב דָּ֑עַת וְשֹׂנֵ֖א תוֹכַ֣חַת בָּֽעַר׃
verses.text_kjv: Whoso loveth instruction loveth knowledge: but he that hateth reproof is brutish.
verses.rendering:
Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge,
but whoever hates correction is senseless.
translator_notes: Musar ('discipline, correction, instruction') is the word for formative training that includes rebuke. Loving musar means welcoming the painful process of being corrected. The sone tokachat ('hater of correction') is ba'ar ('brutish, like an animal, senseless') — a harsh comparison, suggesting that rejecting correction is subhuman.

key_terms.semantic_range: discipline, correction, instruction, chastisement; formative training that reshapes character
key_terms.note: musar is the central educational concept in Proverbs. It encompasses verbal correction, life consequences, and parental/divine training. It is never pleasant in the moment but produces wisdom in the long run.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֗וֹב יָפִ֣יק רָ֭צוֹן מֵיְהוָ֑ה וְאִ֖ישׁ מְזִמּ֣וֹת יַרְשִֽׁיעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: A good man obtaineth favour of the LORD: but a man of wicked devices will he condemn.
verses.rendering:
A good person draws favor from the LORD,
but a person who schemes He condemns.
translator_notes: The verb yafiq ('draws out, obtains, brings forth') suggests that the good person's relationship with God naturally produces ratson ('favor'). The ish mezimmot ('person of plots, schemer') receives condemnation — yarshi'a, from the same root as rasha ('wicked'), meaning God declares him guilty.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יִכּ֣וֹן אָ֭דָם בְּרִשְׁעָ֑ה וְשֹׁ֥רֶשׁ צַ֝דִּיקִ֗ים בַּל־יִמּֽוֹט׃
verses.text_kjv: A man shall not be established by wickedness: but the root of the righteous shall not be moved.
verses.rendering:
No one is established by wickedness,
but the root of the righteous cannot be moved.
translator_notes: The contrast is between instability and rootedness. Wickedness cannot provide yikon ('a firm foundation, an established position'). The righteous have a shoresh ('root') that bal yimmot ('will not be shaken, will not be dislodged'). The botanical metaphor continues from chapter 11: the righteous are rooted; the wicked are rootless.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵֽשֶׁת־חַ֭יִל עֲטֶ֣רֶת בַּעְלָ֑הּ וּכְרָקָ֖ב בְּעַצְמוֹתָ֣יו מְבִישָֽׁה׃
verses.text_kjv: A virtuous woman is a crown to her husband: but she that maketh ashamed is as rottenness in his bones.
verses.rendering:
A woman of strength is a crown to her husband,
but a disgraceful wife is like rot in his bones.
verses.expanded_rendering: The eshet chayil ('woman of valor, woman of strength, capable woman') — the same phrase that opens the famous poem in Proverbs 31:10 — is described as an ateret ('crown, wreath') to her husband. She elevates him, adorns his life, signals his honor to the community. The mevishshah ('one who brings shame, disgraceful one') is the opposite: ke-raqav be-atsmotav ('like decay in his bones'). Bone rot is internal, invisible, progressive, and fatal. A spouse who brings shame does not merely embarrass — she erodes a person from the inside out.
translator_notes: Eshet chayil is notoriously difficult to translate. Chayil means 'strength, valor, capability, wealth, army.' The woman described is not merely 'virtuous' in a passive sense but formidable — strong, competent, and worthy of respect. We render it 'woman of strength' to preserve the active force of the Hebrew.

key_terms.semantic_range: woman of valor, capable woman, woman of substance, worthy woman
key_terms.note: The same phrase opens the acrostic poem in Proverbs 31:10-31. Chayil is used elsewhere for military valor and economic capability. An eshet chayil is a force to be reckoned with — her strength benefits her entire household.

verses.text_hebrew: מַחְשְׁב֣וֹת צַדִּיקִ֣ים מִשְׁפָּ֑ט תַּחְבֻּל֖וֹת רְשָׁעִ֣ים מִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The thoughts of the righteous are right: but the counsels of the wicked are deceit.
verses.rendering:
The plans of the righteous aim at justice;
the schemes of the wicked aim at deceit.
translator_notes: Machshevot ('thoughts, plans, intentions') and tachbulot ('schemes, guidance, steering') are both words for deliberate mental activity. The difference is their orientation: the righteous plan toward mishpat ('justice, right judgment') while the wicked steer toward mirmah ('deceit, fraud').

verses.text_hebrew: דִּבְרֵ֣י רְשָׁעִ֣ים אֱרׇב־דָּ֑ם וּפִ֥י יְ֝שָׁרִ֗ים יַצִּילֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The words of the wicked are to lie in wait for blood: but the mouth of the upright shall deliver them.
verses.rendering:
The words of the wicked ambush for blood,
but the mouth of the upright rescues them.
translator_notes: The wicked's speech is personified as a predator lying in wait (erov dam, 'ambush of blood'). Words become weapons — false testimony, slander, manipulation that leads to someone's destruction. The upright's mouth, by contrast, has saving power — honest speech delivers people from danger.

verses.text_hebrew: הָפ֣וֹךְ רְשָׁעִ֣ים וְאֵינָ֑ם וּבֵ֖ית צַדִּיקִ֣ים יַעֲמֹֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked are overthrown, and are not: but the house of the righteous shall stand.
verses.rendering:
The wicked are overturned and are gone,
but the household of the righteous stands firm.
translator_notes: Hafokh ('overturned, upended') uses the same verb applied to the destruction of Sodom. The wicked are flipped and ve-einam ('and they are not, and they are gone') — total erasure. The beit tsaddiqim ('house of the righteous') — their family, lineage, and legacy — ya'amod ('stands, endures').

verses.text_hebrew: לְפִ֣י שִׂ֭כְלוֹ יְהֻלַּל־אִ֑ישׁ וְנַעֲוֵה־לֵ֝֗ב יִהְיֶ֥ה לָבֽוּז׃
verses.text_kjv: A man shall be commended according to his wisdom: but he that is of a perverse heart shall be despised.
verses.rendering:
A person is praised according to his insight,
but the twisted in heart will be despised.
translator_notes: Sekhel ('insight, prudence, good sense') is the quality that earns a person communal respect. The na'aveh lev ('twisted of heart, warped in mind') — someone whose inner orientation is distorted — will ultimately be despised regardless of outward appearance or temporary success.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֣וֹב נִ֭קְלֶה וְעֶ֣בֶד ל֑וֹ מִ֝מִּתְכַּבֵּ֗ד וַחֲסַר־לָֽחֶם׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is despised, and hath a servant, is better than he that honoureth himself, and lacketh bread.
verses.rendering:
Better to be overlooked and have a servant
than to boast of importance and lack bread.
translator_notes: This 'better than' proverb punctures social pretension. The niqleh ('lightly esteemed, overlooked, despised') who has a servant — meaning he has enough resources to sustain a household — is better off than the mitkabbed ('one who glorifies himself, who puts on airs') who chasar lachem ('lacks bread'). Substance beats performance.

verses.text_hebrew: יוֹדֵ֣עַ צַ֭דִּיק נֶ֣פֶשׁ בְּהֶמְתּ֑וֹ וְֽרַחֲמֵ֥י רְ֝שָׁעִ֗ים אַכְזָרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: A righteous man regardeth the life of his beast: but the tender mercies of the wicked are cruel.
verses.rendering:
A righteous person knows the life of his animal,
but the compassion of the wicked is cruelty.
verses.expanded_rendering: The yode'a tsaddiq nefesh behemto ('the righteous knows the nefesh of his animal') is a remarkable statement. The righteous person pays attention to what his animal needs — its hunger, thirst, fatigue, pain. The verb yode'a ('knows') implies intimate, attentive awareness, not abstract knowledge. This is one of the earliest ethical affirmations of animal welfare in ancient Near Eastern literature. The second line delivers a devastating oxymoron: rachamei resha'im akhzari ('the compassions of the wicked are cruel'). Even when the wicked attempt kindness, the result is cruelty — their character corrupts everything they touch, including mercy itself.
translator_notes: The use of nefesh ('soul, life, being') for the animal is significant. Hebrew does not restrict nefesh to humans — animals are nefesh chayyah ('living beings') in Genesis 1. The righteous person recognizes the animal's experience as real.

The oxymoron 'the compassion of the wicked is cruelty' suggests that wickedness distorts even good impulses. A wicked person's version of mercy may be self-serving, manipulative, or ultimately harmful.

key_terms.semantic_range: soul, life, being, self, appetite, desire, breath; the animating force of a living creature
key_terms.note: nefesh applied to an animal affirms that creatures have an inner life that deserves recognition. The righteous person's morality extends beyond human relationships to include responsibility toward animals in his care.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹבֵ֣ד אַ֭דְמָתוֹ יִֽשְׂבַּֽע־לָ֑חֶם וּמְרַדֵּ֖ף רֵיקִ֣ים חֲסַר־לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: He that tilleth his land shall be satisfied with bread: but he that followeth vain persons is void of understanding.
verses.rendering:
Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread,
but whoever chases empty pursuits lacks sense.
translator_notes: The oved admato ('worker of his ground') is the diligent farmer whose labor produces yisba lachem ('satisfaction of bread, plenty of food'). The meradef reqim ('pursuer of empty things') — one who chases get-rich-quick schemes or idle fantasies — ends up chasar lev ('lacking heart/sense'). Honest work produces; chasing illusions wastes.

verses.text_hebrew: חָמַ֣ד רָ֭שָׁע מְצ֣וֹד רָעִ֑ים וְשֹׁ֖רֶשׁ צַדִּיקִ֣ים יִתֵּֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked desireth the net of evil men: but the root of the righteous yieldeth fruit.
verses.rendering:
The wicked covets the plunder of evil people,
but the root of the righteous bears fruit.
translator_notes: The chamad ('covets, desires intensely') of the wicked is directed at metsod ra'im ('the net/catch/plunder of evil people') — he envies the ill-gotten gains of other wicked people. The righteous, by contrast, have a shoresh ('root') that yitten ('gives, produces, yields') — their productivity comes from depth, not theft.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּפֶ֣שַׁע שְׂ֭פָתַיִם מוֹקֵ֣שׁ רָ֑ע וַיֵּצֵ֖א מִצָּרָ֣ה צַדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked is snared by the transgression of his lips: but the just shall come out of trouble.
verses.rendering:
The wicked is trapped by the sin of his lips,
but the righteous escapes from distress.
translator_notes: The pesha sefatayim ('transgression of lips') becomes a moqesh ('snare, trap') — the wicked's own words ensnare him. The righteous, however, manages to exit (yetse, 'goes out, escapes') from tsarah ('distress, trouble'). Honest speech keeps a person free; dishonest speech binds.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפְּרִ֣י פִי־אִ֭ישׁ יִשְׂבַּע־ט֑וֹב וּגְמ֥וּל יְדֵי־אָ֝דָ֗ם יָשִׁ֥יב [יוּשַׁב] לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A man shall be satisfied with good by the fruit of his mouth: and the recompence of a man's hands shall be rendered unto him.
verses.rendering:
From the fruit of his mouth a person is filled with good,
and the work of his hands comes back to him.
translator_notes: The metaphor of 'fruit of the mouth' treats speech as productive labor — words generate consequences the way trees generate fruit. The parallel line extends the principle to manual work: gemul yede adam ('the dealing/reward of a person's hands') yashiv lo ('returns to him'). Both speech and labor create a return — positive or negative — that circles back to the source.

verses.text_hebrew: דֶּ֣רֶךְ אֱ֭וִיל יָשָׁ֣ר בְּעֵינָ֑יו וְשֹׁמֵ֖עַ לְעֵצָ֣ה חָכָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of a fool is right in his own eyes: but he that hearkeneth unto counsel is wise.
verses.rendering:
The way of a fool seems right to him,
but a wise person listens to advice.
translator_notes: The evil ('fool') suffers from moral blindness: his path appears yashar ('straight, right, correct') be-einav ('in his own eyes'). Self-assessment without external input is unreliable. The wise person, by contrast, is shome'a le-etsah ('one who listens to counsel') — teachability is the hallmark of wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱוִ֗יל בַּ֭יּוֹם יִוָּדַ֣ע כַּעְס֑וֹ וְכֹסֶ֖ה קָל֣וֹן עָרֽוּם׃
verses.text_kjv: A fool's wrath is presently known: but a prudent man covereth shame.
verses.rendering:
A fool's irritation is known at once,
but a shrewd person conceals an insult.
translator_notes: The fool's emotional response is ba-yom yivvade'a ('known on the same day, known immediately') — he cannot contain his anger; it spills out the moment he feels it. The arum ('shrewd, prudent, clever') person, however, koseh qalon ('covers disgrace, conceals the insult') — he absorbs the offense without public reaction, choosing the strategic response over the reflexive one.

verses.text_hebrew: יָפִ֣יחַ אֱ֭מוּנָה יַגִּ֣יד צֶ֑דֶק וְעֵ֖ד שְׁקָרִ֣ים מִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He that speaketh truth sheweth forth righteousness: but a false witness deceit.
verses.rendering:
A truthful witness declares what is right,
but a false witness speaks deceit.
translator_notes: The yafiach emunah ('one who breathes out faithfulness') — the verb suggests that truth flows from this person as naturally as breath. He yaggid tsedeq ('declares what is right, announces justice'). The ed sheqarim ('witness of falsehoods') produces mirmah ('deceit, fraud'). The courtroom setting is implied — reliable versus unreliable testimony.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithfulness, reliability, trustworthiness, firmness, steadfastness; from aman ('to be firm')
key_terms.note: emunah here describes the quality of a witness whose testimony can bear weight. It is not merely 'belief' but demonstrated reliability — what you say matches what is real.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֣שׁ בּ֭וֹטֶה כְּמַדְקְר֣וֹת חָ֑רֶב וּלְשׁ֖וֹן חֲכָמִ֣ים מַרְפֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: There is that speaketh like the piercings of a sword: but the tongue of the wise is health.
verses.rendering:
There is reckless talk that cuts like sword thrusts,
but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
verses.expanded_rendering: The boteh ('one who speaks rashly, blurts out') produces speech that is ke-madqerot charev ('like the stabbings of a sword') — careless words that wound as deeply as a blade. The image is visceral: thoughtless speech draws blood. Against this, the leshon chakhamim ('tongue of the wise') is marpe ('healing, health, cure'). Words have the power to wound or to heal, and the difference lies not in the content alone but in the wisdom or foolishness of the speaker. This verse is foundational for the biblical understanding of speech as a moral act with physical consequences.
translator_notes: The verb boteh is rare and suggests reckless, uncontrolled speech — blurting without thinking. The damage is compared to sword wounds, suggesting that verbal injury can be as real and painful as physical violence.
Marpe ('healing') comes from the root rapha ('to heal, to mend'). The wise person's speech functions as medicine — it repairs what has been damaged.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׂפַת־אֱ֭מֶת תִּכּ֣וֹן לָעַ֑ד וְעַד־אַ֝רְגִּ֗יעָה לְשׁ֣וֹן שָֽׁקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: The lip of truth shall be established for ever: but a lying tongue is but for a moment.
verses.rendering:
Truthful speech endures forever,
but a lying tongue lasts only a moment.
translator_notes: The sefat emet ('lip of truth') tikkon la'ad ('is established forever, stands permanently'). Truth has structural integrity — it does not need to be propped up or maintained because it corresponds to reality. The leshon shaqer ('tongue of falsehood') lasts only ad argi'ah ('until a blink, for a moment') — lies have no staying power because they have no foundation.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭רְמָה בְּלֶב־חֹ֣רְשֵׁי רָ֑ע וּלְיֹעֲצֵ֖י שָׁל֣וֹם שִׂמְחָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Deceit is in the heart of them that imagine evil: but to the counsellors of peace is joy.
verses.rendering:
Deceit fills the heart of those who plot evil,
but those who counsel peace find joy.
translator_notes: The chorshe ra ('plotters of evil, devisers of harm') carry mirmah ('deceit') in their hearts — deception is their inner reality, not just their outward behavior. The yo'atse shalom ('counselors of peace') experience simchah ('joy, gladness'). Working toward shalom — wholeness, well-being, communal harmony — produces inner satisfaction that plotting evil never can.

key_terms.semantic_range: peace, wholeness, completeness, well-being, prosperity, harmony; the state of things being as they should be
key_terms.note: shalom is not merely the absence of conflict but the presence of flourishing. Those who counsel shalom are working to make things whole — to restore right relationships and proper order.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יְאֻנֶּ֣ה לַצַּדִּ֣יק כׇּל־אָ֑וֶן וּ֝רְשָׁעִ֗ים מָ֣לְאוּ רָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: There shall no evil happen to the just: but the wicked shall be filled with mischief.
verses.rendering:
No real harm befalls the righteous,
but the wicked are filled with trouble.
translator_notes: The bold claim that lo ye'unneh la-tsaddiq kol aven ('no evil/mischief befalls the righteous') expresses the confidence of wisdom theology at its most optimistic. The sages are not blind to suffering — but they assert that the righteous person's core is protected even when external circumstances are difficult. The wicked, by contrast, male'u ra ('are filled with evil/trouble') — saturated with the consequences of their own choices.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה שִׂפְתֵי־שָׁ֑קֶר וְעֹשֵׂ֖י אֱמוּנָ֣ה רְצוֹנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lying lips are abomination to the LORD: but they that deal truly are his delight.
verses.rendering:
Lying lips are detestable to the LORD,
but those who act faithfully are His delight.
translator_notes: This verse echoes 11:1 in structure: to'evat Yahweh ('abomination to the LORD') paired with retsono ('His delight'). Lying lips provoke divine revulsion; oseh emunah ('doers of faithfulness, those who act reliably') earn divine pleasure. The God of Proverbs cares intensely about honesty.

verses.text_hebrew: אָדָ֣ם עָ֭רוּם כֹּסֶ֣ה דָּ֑עַת וְלֵ֥ב כְּ֝סִילִ֗ים יִקְרָ֥א אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: A prudent man concealeth knowledge: but the heart of fools proclaimeth foolishness.
verses.rendering:
A shrewd person conceals his knowledge,
but the heart of fools broadcasts foolishness.
translator_notes: The arum ('shrewd, prudent') person koseh da'at ('covers knowledge') — he does not display everything he knows but reveals it strategically. The fool's heart yiqra ivvelet ('calls out, proclaims foolishness') — it cannot help advertising its emptiness. Wisdom knows when to speak and when to withhold.

verses.text_hebrew: יַד־חָרוּצִ֥ים תִּמְשׁ֑וֹל וּ֝רְמִיָּ֗ה תִּהְיֶ֥ה לָמַֽס׃
verses.text_kjv: The hand of the diligent shall bear rule: but the slothful shall be under tribute.
verses.rendering:
The hand of the diligent will rule,
but laziness leads to forced labor.
translator_notes: The charutsim ('diligent, sharp, decisive') — from a root meaning 'to cut, to be sharp' — gain authority (timshol, 'will rule'). The remiyyah ('laziness, slack hand, negligence') leads to mas ('forced labor, tribute labor, corvee'). In the ancient economy, those who failed to manage their own resources ended up working someone else's fields as conscripted labor.

verses.text_hebrew: דְּאָגָ֣ה בְלֶב־אִ֣ישׁ יַשְׁחֶ֑נָּה וְדָבָ֖ר ט֣וֹב יְשַׂמְּחֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Heaviness in the heart of man maketh it stoop: but a good word maketh it glad.
verses.rendering:
Anxiety in a person's heart weighs it down,
but a good word lifts it up.
translator_notes: De'agah ('anxiety, worry, care') in the heart yashchennah ('causes it to bow down, depresses it'). The remedy is simple and powerful: davar tov ('a good word') yesamm'chennah ('makes it glad, lifts it'). The sages recognized the connection between emotional health and speech — the right word at the right time has real therapeutic power.

verses.text_hebrew: יָתֵ֣ר מֵ֭רֵעֵהוּ צַדִּ֑יק וְדֶ֖רֶךְ רְשָׁעִ֣ים תַּתְעֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteous is more excellent than his neighbour: but the way of the wicked seduceth them.
verses.rendering:
The righteous guides his neighbor well,
but the way of the wicked leads them astray.
translator_notes: The verb yater is debated. It may mean 'explores, scouts out' (from tur) — the righteous person scouts the path for his neighbor, serving as a guide. The wicked's path, by contrast, tat'em ('causes them to wander, leads them astray'). The righteous person's moral clarity benefits those around him.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יַחֲרֹ֣ךְ רְמִיָּ֣ה צֵיד֑וֹ וְהוֹן־אָדָ֖ם יָקָ֣ר חָרֽוּץ׃
verses.text_kjv: The slothful man roasteth not that which he took in hunting: but the substance of a diligent man is precious.
verses.rendering:
The lazy person does not roast what he catches,
but diligence is a person's precious wealth.
translator_notes: The image is vivid: the lazy person goes hunting but cannot be bothered to prepare the catch — the meat rots uneaten. All effort is wasted by the failure to follow through. The diligent person's hon ('wealth, substance') is yaqar ('precious, valuable') — not because he has more possessions but because he stewards them responsibly.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֹֽרַח־צְדָקָ֥ה חַיִּ֑ים וְדֶ֖רֶךְ נְתִיבָ֣ה אַל־מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: In the way of righteousness is life; and in the pathway thereof there is no death.
verses.rendering:
On the path of righteousness there is life;
along that road there is no death.
translator_notes: The chapter closes with a grand claim: orach tsedaqah ('the path of righteousness') is chayyim ('life'). The derekh netivah ('the way of the path, that road') is al mavet ('not death, no death'). Righteousness and life are mapped onto the same road. This is the core conviction of the Solomonic collection: the moral structure of reality rewards those who walk rightly.
